text: This video describes how robotic surgery can be very helpful in managing complex biliary duct lesions. The patient is a seventy-six year old female with a biliary structure nine years after laparoscopic polycystectomy. The structure was classified as a bismuth-Strasberg E-tree lesion.

Four robotic 8mm ports and one assistant 12mm port are positioned. During hilar dissection, the right and left hepatic ducts in the stenotic area are clearly visible. Old hemostatic clips are removed from the ductus, and polymer clips near the common hepatic duct and hilum are extracted.
The surgeon transects the left hepatic duct, followed by resection of the common duct below the stenosis using a GIAS tapering device.
The surgeon sutures the right and left hepatic ducts together, creating a double parallel configuration where the ducts are connected side-by-side.

The surgeon constructs the small intestine 30 centimeters distal from the Treitz ligament, pulling the distal limb upwards to prepare for the hepatico-jejunostomy. Using the double barrel technique, an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy anastomosis is performed, with the posterior layers sutured together using a running stitch with absorbable V-lock sutures.

The surgeon sutures the anterior layers of the anastomosis using a V-lock technique, applying gradual tension with two separate sutures. A resorbable two-zero barbed wire is used for the outer layer, while a three-zero barbed wire is used for the inner layer.
A silicon drain is placed in the surgical field. The distal limb of the UNA reconstruction is marked with Chinese ink for endoscopic guidance.

In this table, the perioperative data are shown. Iatrogenic biliary duct injury can cause lifetime disability and chronic liver disease. Due to the advantage of robotic surgery, a precise and accurate reconstruction is possible.
